hero.title: Frequently Asked Questions
hero.description: Everything you need to know about Vyzora's architecture, security, and developer-focused analytics platform.

features.title: Sub-ms Ingestion
features.desc: Events are enqueued in <1ms using our decoupled architecture.

features.title: Privacy First
features.desc: GDPR ready. No PII storage. Full data ownership by default.

features.title: Zero Dependency
features.desc: SDK is <3KB gzipped with zero external dependencies.

questions.question: What is Vyzora?
questions.answer: Vyzora is a modern, high-performance event analytics platform built for engineering teams who need deep insights without sacrificing privacy or performance.

questions.question: How does the data flow work?
questions.answer: It's a streamlined 4-step process: 1. Capture (SDK), 2. Ingest (Scalable API), 3. Queue (Redis/BullMQ), and 4. Persist (PostgreSQL). This decoupled flow ensures your app stays fast while data is processed reliably in the background.

questions.question: Is it easy to integrate?
questions.answer: Yes. Integration takes less than 2 minutes. Just install the zero-dependency SDK, initialize it with your API key, and Vyzora automatically begins tracking page views and sessions.

questions.question: Who is Vyzora built for?
questions.answer: Vyzora is built for developers and product teams who want full control over their data, sub-millisecond tracking latency, and a scalable architecture that grows with their traffic.

questions.question: How does it handle high traffic?
questions.answer: Vyzora is designed for horizontal scaling. By using an asynchronous producer-consumer model, it can handle thousands of events per second by simply adding more API or worker replicas.

questions.question: Why do you use a Queue (Redis)?
questions.answer: To prevent your database from becoming a bottleneck. By enqueuing events first, we can acknowledge the request in less than 1ms and let the background workers handle the heavy lifting of saving to the database.

questions.question: What happens when an event is tracked?
questions.answer: The SDK captures the interaction (like a page view or click), batches it with others, and sends it to our ingestion server. We validate it instantly and push it into a high-speed Redis queue. Workers then pick it up for permanent storage.

questions.question: What technology powers the ingestion flow?
questions.answer: Our stack is built for speed: Nginx for load balancing, Express for the API, Redis and BullMQ for reliable queuing, and Prisma with PostgreSQL for scalable data persistence.

questions.question: Is my data safe during high traffic spikes?
questions.answer: Yes. Our system is designed with a 'Buffer First' approach. Even during massive traffic spikes, events are safely held in our high-speed Redis queue until the workers can process them, ensuring no data loss and zero impact on your application's performance.

questions.question: Why did you choose a monorepo structure?
questions.answer: Shared TypeScript types between SDK, backend, and frontend. Single source of truth for versioning. Atomic commits across multiple packages. Easier dependency management with npm workspaces.

questions.question: Walk me through the event ingestion flow from SDK to database.
questions.answer: SDK batches events → POST to Nginx → Round-robin to API replica → Zod validation → Redis LPUSH → BullMQ worker picks up → Prisma bulk insert → PostgreSQL with composite index.

questions.question: Why did you switch from monolithic to microservices architecture?
questions.answer: Monolithic version had database writes blocking API responses. Decoupling into Producer (API) + Consumer (Worker) achieved sub-millisecond API response by offloading writes to background jobs.

questions.question: How does horizontal scaling work in your system?
questions.answer: Docker Compose with --scale api=N --scale worker=M. Nginx load balances across API replicas. BullMQ distributes jobs across worker replicas. Each service has tuned connection pool limits.

questions.question: Why Redis + BullMQ instead of just a database queue?
questions.answer: Redis LPUSH is <0.5ms vs database INSERT ~10-50ms. BullMQ provides retry logic, exponential backoff, job prioritization, and monitoring out of the box. Better separation of concerns.

questions.question: What happens if Redis goes down?
questions.answer: API returns 500 errors. Events are lost (acknowledged trade-off for speed). Could add: API fallback to direct DB writes, or Redis cluster with replication for HA.

questions.question: How do you prevent duplicate events?
questions.answer: SDK: Full-path guard (pathname + search), tracks lastTrackedPath, only fires on actual navigation changes. Backend: skipDuplicates: true in Prisma createMany.

questions.question: Explain your database indexing strategy.
questions.answer: Composite index on (projectId, createdAt) because all queries filter by project first, then aggregate by time ranges. This makes time-series queries use index-only scans.

questions.question: Why Nginx instead of just Docker's built-in load balancing?
questions.answer: Nginx provides keepalive connection pooling (32 connections), health checks, request buffering, and granular routing control. Better performance for HTTP workloads.

questions.question: How would you handle 10x traffic growth?
questions.answer: Add more API/worker replicas, scale Redis (cluster mode), implement connection pooling at database, add CDN for SDK delivery, consider regional deployments for latency.

questions.question: What's your disaster recovery strategy?
questions.answer: PostgreSQL: Supabase handles automated backups. Redis: Ephemeral queue, acceptable to lose in-flight jobs. Frontend: Vercel has automatic rollbacks. Backend: Docker images tagged by version.

questions.question: Why did you choose Supabase over self-hosted PostgreSQL?
questions.answer: Managed connection pooling (transaction mode on port 6543), automated backups, built-in monitoring, easier to scale vertically, and focus on application logic instead of database operations.

questions.question: Explain the Producer-Consumer pattern in your implementation.
questions.answer: Producer (API): Validates requests, enqueues to Redis, returns immediately. Consumer (Worker): Polls Redis, processes batches, handles retries. Decouples request handling from expensive I/O.

questions.question: How do you ensure workers don't overwhelm the database?
questions.answer: Per-worker connection pool limits (5 connections), bulk inserts reduce transaction overhead, workers process jobs sequentially with concurrency limits, monitoring Redis queue depth.

questions.question: What's the maximum events per second your system can handle?
questions.answer: Benchmarked ~4500 req/s on API gateway (M2 Pro, local). Production bottleneck would be worker consumption rate, depends on database IOPS. Scalable by adding workers.

questions.question: Why separate "backend" and "backend-scalable" folders?
questions.answer: Legacy monolithic version kept for backwards compatibility and simpler deployment scenarios. Scalable version is production-recommended. Allows gradual migration.

questions.question: How does session reconstruction work?
questions.answer: Lazy reconstruction at query time. Group events by sessionId, calculate MIN(createdAt) for start, MAX(createdAt) for end, COUNT(*) for depth. No session objects stored.

questions.question: Why store session/visitor IDs in localStorage instead of cookies?
questions.answer: No server-side overhead, no HTTP header bloat, works in cross-domain scenarios, persists across tab closures, and SDK controls lifecycle directly.

questions.question: What trade-offs did you make for performance?
questions.answer: Lost events if Redis crashes (no persistence), eventually consistent writes (async workers), no strict deduplication (accepted minor duplicates), simplified session model.

questions.question: How would you add real-time analytics?
questions.answer: WebSocket connection from dashboard to backend, Redis Pub/Sub for event broadcasts, worker publishes to channel after DB write, frontend subscribes and updates metrics live.

questions.question: Why is your SDK <3KB gzipped?
questions.answer: Zero dependencies, vanilla TypeScript, tree-shakeable ESM build, aggressive minification with tsup, batching reduces payload size, no polyfills.

questions.question: Explain the atomic flushing mechanism.
questions.answer: Use splice(0, queue.length) to atomically clear the queue BEFORE transport. If transport fails, events are already gone (acceptable loss). Prevents race conditions during concurrent flushes.

questions.question: How do you prevent double-flushing?
questions.answer: Boolean flushing flag set before transport, cleared in finally block. Guards against simultaneous visibilitychange + pagehide events firing and triggering multiple flushes.

questions.question: Why sendBeacon first, then fetch with keepalive?
questions.answer: sendBeacon is designed for page unload (queued by browser, survives page close). fetch with keepalive: true is fallback for when sendBeacon unavailable or fails.

questions.question: How does SPA navigation tracking work?
questions.answer: Wrap history.pushState and history.replaceState using monkey-patching. Listen to popstate. Fire pageview only when full path (pathname + search) changes, ignore hash-only changes.

questions.question: What happens in private browsing mode?
questions.answer: localStorage blocked → SDK catches exception → uses in-memory fallback for visitor ID. Session works identically. No crashes, graceful degradation.

questions.question: Why 30-minute session timeout?
questions.answer: Industry standard (Google Analytics uses 30 min). Balances between session continuity and accurate bounce rate. Configurable via SDK but defaults to 30 min.

questions.question: How do you handle SDK initialization race conditions?
questions.answer: Singleton guard (historyWrapped flag) ensures window.history patched exactly once. Multiple SDK instances share global registry. First instance wins.

questions.question: Why batch at 20 events OR 10 seconds?
questions.answer: 20 events: Prevents unbounded queue growth on high-traffic sites. 10 seconds: Ensures timely delivery on low-traffic sites. Whichever comes first.

questions.question: Explain the retry logic?
questions.answer: Single retry for 5xx or network errors only. Never retry 4xx (client error, won't succeed). No re-insertion into queue (events lost on persistent failure). Prevents infinite loops.

questions.question: How does the SDK handle disabled state?
questions.answer: When enabled: false, SDK is completely inert. No listeners attached, no history patching, no localStorage access, no network calls. Zero overhead.

questions.question: Why use tsup instead of webpack/rollup?
questions.answer: tsup is zero-config, built on esbuild (fast), handles ESM + CJS dual output automatically, tree-shaking by default, perfect for libraries.

questions.question: How do you ensure type safety between SDK and backend?
questions.answer: Shared Zod schemas in monorepo. SDK imports validation types from backend package. Type mismatches caught at compile time.

questions.question: Why auto-capture metadata instead of requiring user input?
questions.answer: Reduces integration friction (just initialize SDK). Most metadata (browser, OS, screen) is universally useful. Users can extend via custom properties.

questions.question: How does the dashboard handle real-time updates?
questions.answer: Currently polling with React Query (refetch interval). Future: WebSocket connection with server-sent events for live metrics.

questions.question: Why SHA-256 hash API keys instead of storing plain text?
questions.answer: If database compromised, attackers can't use keys to ingest fake data. One-way hashing means keys are validate-only, not retrievable.

questions.question: How do you prevent API key leakage?
questions.answer: Keys only returned once on project creation. Never returned in list endpoints. Frontend never stores keys (user copies immediately). Rate limiting prevents brute force.

questions.question: Explain the CSRF protection on logout.
questions.answer: Logout requires Referer or Origin header matching backend domain. Prevents attacker's site from making cross-origin logout requests to force user re-authentication.

questions.question: Why validate JWT_SECRET at startup?
questions.answer: Prevents deploying with default/weak secrets. Server crashes on boot if JWT_SECRET is "your-secret-key" or empty. Fail-fast for security misconfigurations.

questions.question: How do you prevent prototype pollution in event metadata?
questions.answer: Zod schema enforces scalar values only (string, number, boolean). No nested objects allowed in metadata. Prevents __proto__ injection attacks.

questions.question: Why 500-event cap per request?
questions.answer: Prevents DoS via massive payloads. 500 events is generous for batching but prevents multi-MB requests. HTTP body size also limited to 2MB at Nginx.

questions.question: How does rate limiting work?
questions.answer: express-rate-limit with different limits per route: Auth (5/min), Project Creation (10/hr), Ingest (1000/min), Metrics (100/min). Sliding window, IP-based.

questions.question: Why HttpOnly cookies for JWT?
questions.answer: JavaScript cannot access HttpOnly cookies, preventing XSS attacks from stealing tokens. Cookie automatically sent with requests, no localStorage exposure.

questions.question: Explain the CORS strategy.
questions.answer: Ingestion endpoint: Reflected origin (accepts all). Dashboard endpoints: Strict whitelist (frontend URL only). Different policies for different security contexts.

questions.question: How do you prevent SQL injection?
questions.answer: Prisma uses parameterized queries. Raw SQL uses bound parameters ($1, $2). Never string concatenation. Zod validates all inputs before queries.

questions.question: Why no PII in default tracking?
questions.answer: GDPR compliance by design. Visitor/Session IDs are random UUIDs, no user emails/names. IP addresses not stored. User can opt-in to track user IDs via identify().

questions.question: How do you handle security vulnerabilities in dependencies?
questions.answer: Automated npm audit in CI/CD. Manual patches when high/critical found (Axios, Next.js, express-rate-limit). Lock files prevent unexpected updates.

questions.question: Why suppress stack traces in production?
questions.answer: Prevents leaking internal database schema, file paths, environment details to attackers. Generic 500 errors only. Detailed logs go to monitoring, not API responses.

questions.question: How would you implement API key rotation?
questions.answer: Allow multiple active keys per project, add expires_at field, require old key for generating new, grace period where both work, then invalidate old.

questions.question: What's your approach to input validation?
questions.answer: Zod schemas at API boundary, validate before business logic, fail fast with 400 + field-level errors, whitelist approach (only allow known fields).

questions.question: Why LRU cache for API keys with 5-minute TTL?
questions.answer: API key validation is database lookup on every ingest request. Caching eliminates 95%+ of DB queries. 5 min balances freshness (key revocation) with hit rate.

questions.question: Why is project ownership cache only 5 seconds?
questions.answer: Ownership checked on analytics queries (less frequent than ingest). 5 sec prevents race conditions when user creates/deletes projects. Shorter than API key cache.

questions.question: How did you optimize the analytics queries?
questions.answer: Replaced ORM with raw SQL for aggregations, composite index on (projectId, createdAt), parallel execution with Promise.all, single batch endpoint instead of 6 separate calls.

questions.question: Explain the 80% network reduction claim.
questions.answer: Originally 6 sequential requests: pageviews, visitors, sessions, top pages, top events, browser stats. Consolidated into 1 batch request returning all metrics.

questions.question: Why batch inserts instead of individual inserts?
questions.answer: createMany sends single SQL statement with multiple VALUES rows. Reduces round-trips, transaction overhead, index updates. ~10x faster for bulk operations.

questions.question: How does connection pooling work?
questions.answer: Prisma maintains pool of reusable connections. Workers configured for 5 connections each. Prevents "too many clients" error. Pool exhaustion causes queuing, not crashes.

questions.question: Why compression middleware?
questions.answer: Gzip responses reduce bandwidth by 70-90% for JSON. Especially valuable for analytics responses (large arrays). Minimal CPU cost, huge transfer savings.

questions.question: How do you prevent N+1 queries?
questions.answer: Use COUNT(DISTINCT) aggregations instead of fetching all records. Prisma include with caution. Raw SQL for complex joins. Dataloader pattern for batch fetching.

questions.question: Why timezone casting at database layer?
questions.answer: PostgreSQL can cast timestamptz to specific timezone during aggregation. Eliminates client-side timezone math, ensures consistent day boundaries, leverages DB's optimized date functions.

questions.question: How would you optimize for 10M events/day?
questions.answer: Partition events table by date, archive old data to cold storage, materialized views for common aggregations, read replicas for analytics, separate OLAP database.

questions.question: Why Nginx keepalive connection pooling?
questions.answer: Reuses TCP connections to upstream API servers. Eliminates handshake overhead. 32 persistent connections handle thousands of requests. Reduces latency by ~20-40ms.

questions.question: Explain your database index strategy for time-series data.
questions.answer: Composite index (projectId, createdAt) allows index-only scans for queries like "events in last 30 days for project X". PostgreSQL can skip table access entirely.

questions.question: Why async/await instead of callbacks in worker?
questions.answer: Cleaner error handling, easier to read sequential logic, prevents callback hell, works well with Prisma promises, allows try/catch for graceful failure.

questions.question: How do you monitor queue health?
questions.answer: Log warning if Redis queue depth >1000 items. Indicates workers falling behind. Trigger: add more workers, investigate slow queries, check database CPU.

questions.question: What's the cold start time for your backend?
questions.answer: ~2-3 seconds: Load environment, initialize Prisma, connect to Redis, compile TypeScript (if dev). Production Docker image is pre-built, so <1 second.

questions.question: Walk me through your CI/CD pipeline.
questions.answer: Push → GitHub Actions triggers → Parallel jobs (lint, build, test, security audit) for each workspace → Vercel auto-deploys frontend → Manual EC2 deploy for backend.

questions.question: Why separate lint and build jobs?
questions.answer: Fail fast on lint errors (cheap), don't waste time building. Parallel execution saves time. Different teams might own different jobs in production.

questions.question: How do you handle environment variables in deployment?
questions.answer: Root .env for local dev. Vercel dashboard for frontend env vars. EC2 uses systemd environment files. GitHub Secrets for CI/CD. Never commit secrets.

questions.question: Why Docker instead of deploying directly to EC2?
questions.answer: Reproducible builds, dependency isolation, easy rollbacks (tag versions), works locally and in prod identically, scales horizontally with docker-compose.

questions.question: Explain your versioning strategy.
questions.answer: Semantic versioning (major.minor.patch). Root version tracks overall system. Each package (SDK, backend, frontend) has independent version. Synchronized via changelogs.

questions.question: How do you handle database migrations in production?
questions.answer: prisma migrate deploy in CI/CD before app deployment. Migrations are forward-only, additive changes preferred. Backwards-compatible schema changes to avoid downtime.

questions.question: Why 68 deployments in 8 weeks?
questions.answer: Continuous deployment. Every merged PR triggers deploy. Bug fixes, features, security patches. Averages to ~1 deployment per day. Rapid iteration.

questions.question: How do you rollback a bad deployment?
questions.answer: Vercel: One-click rollback to previous deployment. EC2: Deploy previous Docker image tag. Database: Migrations are harder, requires backup restoration.

questions.question: What's in your .gitignore?
questions.answer: node_modules, .env, .next, dist, build, Prisma generated client, logs, OS files (.DS_Store), IDE configs.

questions.question: Why npm workspaces instead of Lerna/Turborepo?
questions.answer: Native npm support (npm 7+), no extra dependencies, simple for this scale. Turborepo overkill for 3 packages. Lerna is deprecated.

questions.question: How do you ensure builds are reproducible?
questions.answer: Lock files (package-lock.json), Docker base images pinned to specific versions, Prisma schema versioned, Node.js version specified in .nvmrc.

questions.question: Why Vitest instead of Jest?
questions.answer: Faster (Vite-powered), native ESM support, better TypeScript integration, less configuration, modern API. Jest works but Vitest is future.

questions.question: How do you test the SDK?
questions.answer: Unit tests for queue logic, storage wrappers, metadata extraction. Integration tests with mock API. Manual testing in browser for SPA navigation.

questions.question: What's your monitoring strategy?
questions.answer: Currently: Application logs, Vercel analytics, PostgreSQL metrics from Supabase. Future: Add Sentry for errors, Datadog for APM, Grafana dashboards for Redis/queue metrics.

questions.question: How would you implement blue-green deployment?
questions.answer: Run two identical EC2 environments. Deploy to "green" while "blue" serves traffic. Smoke test green. Switch Nginx upstream to green. Keep blue for quick rollback.

questions.question: Why limit users to 50 projects?
questions.answer: Prevent abuse, encourage cleanup of unused projects, simplifies UI (no pagination needed), reduces database bloat, aligns with pricing model (future paid tiers).

questions.question: How does the delete confirmation modal improve UX?
questions.answer: Replaces browser's ugly window.confirm(). Styled consistently with app theme. Shows project name for confirmation. Prevents accidental deletions.

questions.question: Why separate "pageview" and custom "events"?
questions.answer: Pageviews are automatic, standard across all sites. Custom events are user-defined actions (signups, purchases). Different aggregation needs and use cases.

questions.question: How would you implement funnels?
questions.answer: Define sequence of events (e.g., "view_product → add_to_cart → purchase"). Query events grouped by sessionId, calculate conversion at each step, aggregate across users.

questions.question: Why auto-collect browser/OS instead of letting users specify?
questions.answer: User-agent parsing is consistent, accurate, no integration effort. Users might misconfigure. Auto-capture ensures data quality across all implementations.

questions.question: How does the changelog modal work?
questions.answer: versions.json stores release history. Modal fetches on button click, renders version list, shows details for selected version. No backend required.

questions.question: Why GitHub OAuth instead of email/password?
questions.answer: No password management, no "forgot password" flow, leverages trusted identity provider, developer-focused audience already has GitHub, reduces security surface.

questions.question: How would you add team collaboration features?
questions.answer: Add Team model, many-to-many with Users, project ownership by team, invite system with email tokens, role-based permissions (owner/admin/viewer).

questions.question: Why show top pages instead of all pages?
questions.answer: Most sites have long tail of low-traffic pages. Top 10 pages cover 80%+ of traffic. Keeps UI focused, reduces cognitive load, faster queries.

questions.question: How would you implement data export?
questions.answer: CSV endpoint: GET /api/projects/:id/export?start=X&end=Y. Stream CSV rows from cursor-based pagination. Background job for large exports, email download link.

questions.question: What was the hardest bug to fix?
questions.answer: Timezone drift causing day buckets to misalign between backend aggregation and frontend rendering. Required understanding PostgreSQL timezone functions and JavaScript Date behavior.

questions.question: What would you do differently if starting over?
questions.answer: Start with microservices architecture from day one. Add comprehensive tests earlier. Use TypeScript strict mode from start. Plan for observability (logging/monitoring) upfront.

questions.question: Why did you build this instead of using Google Analytics?
questions.answer: Privacy-first requirement, no third-party trackers, full data ownership, learning experience, developer-friendly API, can self-host.

questions.question: What's missing that you'd add next?
questions.answer: Real-time analytics via WebSockets, user retention cohorts, A/B testing framework, alerts/anomaly detection, data export API, team collaboration, custom dashboards.

questions.question: How did you handle scope creep?
questions.answer: MVP first (ingestion + basic metrics), then iterate. Documented features in CHANGELOG. Resisted adding complexity until core was solid. Feedback-driven roadmap.

questions.question: What metrics do you track for Vyzora itself?
questions.answer: Vyzora tracking Vyzora (dogfooding). Monitor: SDK load times, API response latency, error rates, deployment frequency, test coverage.

questions.question: How would you monetize this?
questions.answer: Freemium: 10K events/month free. Paid tiers: higher limits, team features, custom domains, data retention, priority support, SLA guarantees.

questions.question: What feedback surprised you most?
questions.answer: Users wanted mobile responsiveness more than advanced analytics. Taught me: solve existing problem perfectly before adding features.

questions.question: How did you prioritize features?
questions.answer: Impact vs. effort matrix. Security/performance > features. User-reported bugs > nice-to-haves. Foundation (auth, ingestion) before polish (charts, animations).

questions.question: What's the most important lesson from this project?
questions.answer: Ship early, iterate based on real usage. 68 deployments taught more than any tutorial. Production bugs drive deep learning. Documentation is code too.

cta.title: Still have questions?
cta.description: Can't find what you're looking for? Reach out to our engineering team.
